summary:
Yam cultivation in West Africa (Dioscorea species, particularly Dioscorea rotundata) represents one of the earliest plant domestication processes in sub-Saharan Africa, though the exact timing remains difficult to determine due to the poor preservation of yam remains in tropical archaeological contexts. Current estimates suggest that African yam cultivation began approximately 8,000–6,000 BCE in the woodland-savanna transition zone of West Africa, making it potentially one of the earliest domestications in the African continent.

The domestication of yams in West Africa was part of a broader transition from intensive foraging to food production in the forest-savanna mosaic environments of the region, where the [[event:17|African Humid Period]] expanded forest margins and the diversity of exploitable plant resources. West African yam cultivation does not fit the model of a single domestication event producing major morphological changes in a short timeframe; instead, it appears to have been a prolonged process of intensifying management of wild yam populations, gradually selecting for larger tubers and more accessible growth habits. Together with oil palm management and the harvesting of other forest-edge plants, early yam cultivation contributed to the foundations of the diverse and ecologically sophisticated agricultural systems that would eventually support the complex societies of West Africa's forest zone.
